title: Harden runner dispatch failures before bundle creation and supervise worker process exits
description:
E2E finding from pi-2m1.27.12 and user feedback.

Observed:
- pi-2m1.27.12 failed dispatch attempts before any run bundle was created.
- .pi/runner/state.json recorded attempts.pi-2m1.27.12.lastError as `"'bundle'"`, implying scheduler error handling assumed a failed dispatch result included a `bundle` key.
- No .pi/runs/runner-pi-2m1.27.12-* bundle exists, so the orchestrator had to inspect runner state to understand the failure.
- User noted that if the subagent runs in a process, the runner should monitor process exit because that catches cases where Beads is not updated.

Required architecture:
- Bundle creation failures and pre-bundle dispatch failures must be recorded explicitly, not as KeyError('bundle').
- Worker process supervision should be the primary execution completion source: record workerPid/process group/start time, monitor exit, write terminal result/live status, then attempt Beads closeout.
- If Beads update fails, run bundle remains terminal with a closeout-failed status/detail.
- On restart, runner reconciles active runs by checking persisted process identity and bundle status, avoiding stuck active runs.
acceptance_criteria:
- Failed run_work/dispatch paths that happen before bundle creation record a clear attempt error and/or failure artifact without KeyError('bundle').
- Worker invocations are process-supervised with PID/process-group/start-time or equivalent safe process identity.
- Process exit always writes terminal result.yaml and live/status.json before Beads closeout.
- If Beads close/update fails after worker exit, the run remains terminal and status exposes closeout failure.
- Restart reconciliation handles active runs whose worker process is alive, exited, or ambiguous.
- Tests cover pre-bundle failure, process exit with Beads update failure, timeout kill, restart reconciliation, and active status reporting.

title: Add event-driven runner wait/watch interface for orchestrators
description:
E2E orchestration gap: the orchestrator currently waits for worker completion with fixed sleeps or ad hoc polling. The user correctly noted this should use an asynchronous/event-driven pattern.

Desired pattern:
- Runner emits lifecycle events: run_started, live_status_updated, run_finished, run_failed, bead_closed, blocker_created.
- CLI exposes `agnt work runner wait --bead <id> --run <id> --timeout <duration> --json`.
- ticket_gateway exposes structured wait/watch or compact event retrieval so the orchestrator can wait without bash sleeps.
- TUI can subscribe to the same event stream.
- Wait should return terminal run result, run bundle path, artifact paths, status, model, cost/context metrics, and any blocker/error.

Observed during Edabit E2E: assistant used `sleep N` before status checks because status is available but no durable completion event/wait tool is exposed to the orchestrator.
acceptance_criteria:
- Runner records run lifecycle events durably and exposes them through CLI/gateway.
- `agnt work runner wait --bead <id> --timeout <duration> --json` blocks until terminal state or timeout without fixed sleeps.
- Wait output includes runId, bundle, status, result summary, artifact paths, active/terminal state, model, cost/context metrics, and blocker/error if any.
- Tests cover success, failure, timeout, already-terminal run, and runner restart with persisted events.
- E2E orchestrator uses wait/watch instead of fixed sleeps.

Root cause hypothesis: routing does not distinguish tasks that require reliable tool execution from read-only prose review tasks. Gemma/openrouter-localish may be acceptable for no-tool smoke output, but is not reliable for command-running verification workers in this harness/tool protocol.

Desired behavior: if an invocation needs command/tool execution, select a tool-reliable model/family or enforce a model capability constraint; mark unresolved-tool-call outcomes as negative routing feedback for the selected model/task/action combination.
acceptance_criteria:

title: Add tail-friendly worker run logs and explicit lessons artifacts
description:
E2E gap confirmed by user during Edabit orchestration test. Requirement: subagents/workers should place all output, including session logs and lessons learned, into a findable directory that can be tailed or examined by the orchestrator.

Observed gap from pi-2m1.27 Stage 4 / pi-2m1.27.4:
- Run bundle existed at `.pi/runs/runner-pi-2m1.27.4-20260709215423`.
- While the worker was active, status exposed only active bead/bundle, not live session progress or a tailable log path.
- Response artifact became available only after timeout and contained a huge raw event stream, not a concise live session log.
- No standard `lessons.md`, `handoff.md`, or `status.json` artifact existed for incremental progress/failure diagnosis.

Desired bundle shape:
```
.pi/runs/<run-id>/
  invocation.yaml
  result.yaml
  live/
    session.jsonl      # append-only, tail-safe worker/session events
    status.json        # current model, phase, last activity/tool, timeout deadline
  artifacts/
    prompt.md
    response.md
    stderr.txt
    metrics.json
    lessons.md         # explicit worker-reported lessons, even if empty
    handoff.md         # concise progress/failure state
```

Runner/orchestrator status should expose these paths so the orchestrator can diagnose active workers without ad hoc process inspection or waiting for timeout.
acceptance_criteria:
- Every worker run creates a stable live log directory before model invocation starts.
- Worker/session events are appended to a tail-friendly log while the worker is running.
- Runner status exposes the run bundle, live log path, current model, current phase, last activity/tool if known, and timeout deadline.
- Each run produces explicit lessons and handoff artifacts, even if empty or marked unavailable due to timeout.
- Timeout/failure result summarizes where to inspect partial logs.
- E2E test verifies the orchestrator can find and inspect these artifacts during an active run without bash/process inspection workarounds.

title: Read-only verify workers need command execution and timeout-safe transcript handling
description:
E2E finding from pi-2m1.27 Stage 4. The verify worker pi-2m1.27.4 was asked to run a read-only pytest command against the epic worktree. Runner selected openrouter-localish/google/gemma-4-31b-it and launched the worker. The run timed out after 300s with exit code 124.

Evidence:
- Bundle: .pi/runs/runner-pi-2m1.27.4-20260709215423
- invocation.yaml selected action=verify, role=verifier, selectedModel=openrouter-localish/google/gemma-4-31b-it, worktreePolicy=none.
- prompt asked the worker to run `/Users/hays/Projects/pi-setup/.venv/bin/python -m pytest e2e-test/test_edabit_challenges.py -q` from `.worktrees/epic/pi-2m1`.
- result.yaml status=failed, exit code 124, completedAt=2026-07-09T21:59:25Z.
- stderr says `pi invocation timed out after 300s`.
- response artifact is ~906k chars of session/event stream and shows the model began a tool call to `read` for the verification skill, but no final verification result.

Hypothesis/root cause:
- Read-only/review/verify workers appear to lack `bash`, so a verifier cannot execute non-mutating verification commands even when the task explicitly requires command evidence.
- The worker should either have a safe command execution capability for read-only verification or the run planner should reject/route such tasks differently before dispatch.
- Timeout artifacts are too noisy and do not summarize partial progress/failure cause clearly.
- The bead remained in_progress despite terminal failed run result, which may require better failed-run closeout/blocker behavior.

Desired behavior:
- Verify actions that require command evidence get a safe read-only shell/test execution mode, or dispatch validation blocks them with a precise handoff.
- Timeout result summarizes last tool/action and preserves compact partial transcript.
- Failed read-only runs transition the bead to a terminal failed/blocked state or create a clear retry/fallback blocker rather than leaving it in_progress.
- Status/TUI shows current model and timeout deadline/progress for active workers.
acceptance_criteria:
- Deterministic regression test reproduces a verify task requiring a read-only command.
- Runner either grants safe bash/test execution for verify or blocks dispatch with a precise handoff before starting a model worker.
- Timeout artifacts include compact cause/last activity rather than only large raw transcript.
- A terminal failed verify run updates Beads state or creates a retry/fallback blocker.
- Existing read-only safety constraints remain: no edits/commits/push/delete.
- Relevant runner/runs/invoke tests pass.

title: Support dependent implementation continuation after dirty epic worktree checkpoint
description:
E2E finding from pi-2m1.27 Edabit workflow. Stage 2 implementation succeeded in `.worktrees/epic/pi-2m1` and left expected uncommitted `e2e-test/` changes. Stage 3 depended on those changes, but runner blocked dispatch because the epic worktree was dirty and created blocker pi-ada. The safety check is correct for unrelated/concurrent work, but the orchestration lifecycle lacks a first-class continuation/checkpoint path for serial dependent implementation stages.

Desired behavior: when a dependency stage succeeds and a dependent implementation stage is ready, tooling should either automatically kick the dependent work back to the runner with an explicit continuation mode, or produce a clear orchestrator action with preserved state and recovery options. Better: the underlying tooling should detect the pattern, record a lesson/issue automatically, and choose a safe default when policy allows.

Possible design:
- Add a dependent-stage policy to metadata.pi/worktreePolicy, e.g. `continuation: same-worktree-after-success` or `checkpointPolicy: dirty-worktree-continuation`.
- Runner distinguishes expected dirty worktree from prior successful dependency vs unsafe dirty worktree from unrelated/untracked changes.
- Runner active/ready status explains: preserved changes, source run, dependent bead, allowed next actions.
- If continuation is safe and approved, dispatch dependent stage in same dirty epic worktree.
- If not safe, create a Beads-backed blocker with options: continue in dirty worktree, checkpoint-integrate, spawn continuation worktree, or defer.
- Automatically capture a lesson/follow-up when the runner encounters this missing lifecycle path.

This avoids manual shell workaround and prevents the orchestrator from inventing ad hoc checkpoint integration.
acceptance_criteria:
- A deterministic test reproduces: implementation stage A succeeds and leaves expected changes in epic worktree; dependent implementation stage B is ready.
- Runner does not silently treat this as an ordinary dirty-worktree failure.
- Runner either dispatches B under an explicit approved continuation policy or creates a precise Beads-backed handoff with preserved state, options, and recovery path.
- Status/TUI/gateway expose the continuation/blocker state clearly.
- A lesson/follow-up artifact is recorded automatically or through a structured runner event.
- No unsafe concurrent write dispatch into dirty worktrees.
- Existing dirty-worktree safety remains for unrelated or conflicting changes.

title: Execute critical harness self-recovery fixes with valid epic worktree policy
description: Execution bead to unblock the runner after live dry-run showed the previous high-priority repair beads were dispatchable by orchestration metadata but blocked by worktree policy (`existing-worktree`), while the scheduler currently requires `epic-worktree` for implementation work. Implement the critical no-manual-shell harness fixes together: drained-runner self-recovery, autonomous daemon dispatch without manual tick, and accurate tick timeout/status behavior. This bead supersedes the malformed-dispatch execution metadata on pi-2m1.16, pi-2m1.17, and pi-2m1.18 if selected by the runner.
acceptance_criteria: TDD evidence recorded. The runner/orchestrator recovers from drained state on normal startup without manual shell reset. The daemon autonomously dispatches approved ready work on its configured interval without manual `runner tick`. Tick timeout/status handling no longer reports a live-but-slow service as `not-running`. The implementation uses the required epic-worktree policy, records RED/GREEN evidence, and passes relevant orchestrator, runner client/service/scheduler tests plus project checks appropriate to the touched files. Follow-ups pi-2m1.16, pi-2m1.17, and pi-2m1.18 are reconciled as fixed/superseded.
notes:
Repair-mode closeout evidence (user approved pi-a2u): RED tests failed for metadata action dispatch, blocked-ready scanning, tick timeout classification, resume-undrain behavior, auto scheduler, and orchestrator scheduler-aware startup. GREEN: focused pytest for runner/orchestrator paths passed (32 passed); relevant runner/orchestrator suite passed (40 passed); full verification passed: scripts/check-pi-config.sh and .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q (187 passed); bash -n scripts/*.sh, routing-smoke, role-context-smoke, and git diff --check passed. Fixed behavior: dispatch_plan and scheduler use metadata.pi.action; scheduler scans past blocked ready items; runner resume clears draining; daemon serve starts an auto scheduler and reports schedulerEnabled; client timeouts no longer report not-running; orchestrator resumes drained services and upgrades old non-scheduler services with activeRuns=0.
Additional hardening after closeout: scheduler now skips unmanaged ready items without creating blockers, preventing autonomous daemon noise on generic backlog. Re-verified full suite: scripts/check-pi-config.sh and .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q (188 passed), bash -n scripts/*.sh, routing-smoke, role-context-smoke, git diff --check. Deployed tracked pi/ config to ~/.pi with scripts/bootstrap-pi-config.sh --apply and verified live config. Restarted project runner with new code: daemon started, running=true, acceptingNewWork=true, draining=false, schedulerEnabled=true, activeRuns=[]; dry-run tick skips unmanaged pi-ede/pi-a7w instead of creating blockers.
Follow-up created after verification: pi-2m1.20 tracks the remaining implementation-worker bootstrap blocker (runner must launch approved implement workers in the epic worktree with write-capable/no-orchestrator tools instead of text-only/default Pi invocation).
close_reason: Implemented critical harness self-recovery fixes in repair mode with RED/GREEN tests and full verification evidence.

title: Resolve pi-archimedes Ctrl+V shortcut conflict with built-in image paste
description: Pi reports that user package npm:pi-archimedes registers Ctrl+V, conflicting with built-in app.clipboard.pasteImage; Pi currently chooses pi-archimedes. Diagnose installed package behavior and Pi keybinding/extension APIs, decide whether Archimedes should use a different configurable shortcut or defer to the built-in binding, add regression/config checks, and preserve both image paste and Archimedes functionality. This is a non-dispatching follow-up after the repair-tool spike; do not modify package/runtime config yet.
acceptance_criteria: Conflict reproduced and source ownership confirmed; selected binding behavior recorded; no startup warning; built-in image paste and Archimedes action remain reachable; tracked config/package fix verified and separately approved before deployment.

title: Prevent stale extension ctx crash in direct agnt invoke review sessions
description: During pi-2m1.27.84 independent follow-up review, direct `agnt invoke openai-codex/gpt-5.6-sol ...` produced a model response but then crashed in deployed orchestrator-service.ts: showFailure accessed ctx.ui after session replacement/reload and ExtensionRunner.assertActive raised the documented stale-context error. Evidence: .pi/reviews/pi-2m1.27.84/followup-action-gate.stderr.txt and invocation metric record 3c810fe016995e5c. Diagnose extension async callbacks across session replacement; prevent showFailure/handleAsyncFailure from dereferencing stale captured ctx; preserve actionable error evidence without crashing the invoking process. This is a non-dispatching follow-up only; do not implement or deploy from this draft.
acceptance_criteria: Behavioral regression reproduces a late async failure after session replacement/reload without using stale ctx.ui; direct agnt invoke exits according to worker outcome rather than extension stale-context crash; error remains observable; existing orchestrator lifecycle tests pass.

title: Final live semantic terminal OK witness

Return exactly one semantic marker: OK: final controlled semantic-terminal success witness; evidence: intentional validation success. Include no line beginning with ERROR.
acceptance_criteria: Runs autonomously after the priority-0 ERROR witness; process exit 0 with semanticOutcome=ok and effective exit 0; result/live/handoff status succeeded; response preserved; read-only Bead auto-closes; no failure blocker or attempt state; runner idle; no workspace mutation, manual tick, restart, or repair mode.

title: Add structured audited requeue for retry-exhausted runner work
description: The original Stage B pi-2m1.27.66 exhausted retry attempts on a checkpoint bug that was subsequently fixed and deployed. After its human hold gate was released, normal orchestration had no safe structured operation to clear/reset only that bead's retry state, so a replacement bead was required. Add a Beads/gateway/runner-owned requeue operation that validates the target and human authorization where consequential, clears only the target's retry/backoff state transactionally, emits durable before/after evidence and lifecycle events, and lets the autonomous scheduler reconsider it without manual tick, daemon restart, raw state edit, or shell workaround.
acceptance_criteria: A structured operation can requeue exactly one eligible retry-deferred or retry-exhausted bead after validation; it transactionally clears only that bead's attempts and records caller authorization, prior attempt state, reason, timestamp, and lifecycle event; implementation/write work requires human authorization and cannot be invoked through a model-only approval bypass; no raw state edit, manual tick, or daemon restart is needed; autonomous next interval considers the target; invalid, active, terminal, unmanaged, or unrelated targets are rejected precisely; gateway/status expose outcome; focused and full verification passes.

title: Preserve Bead approvalRefs and inputRefs in implementation run artifacts
description: Fresh Stage A run .pi/runs/runner-pi-2m1.27.63-20260710044101 dispatched only after human-UI approval pi-5c8 and the worker itself verified that approval, but result.yaml recorded approvalRefs=[] and invocation.yaml recorded inputRefs=[] despite metadata declaring plan and Stage 1 references. Trace Bead metadata snapshot/dispatch bundle propagation so resolved human approval references and declared inputRefs are preserved durably. Do not weaken human-only approval validation.
acceptance_criteria: Regression reproduces missing target approvalRefs/inputRefs; invocation and terminal result preserve verified human approval identifiers and declared input references without trusting model-supplied approval state; existing human-only approval integrity remains intact; focused/full tests pass.

title: Preserve scheduler checkpoint.json when initializing successor run bundle
description: During normal dispatch of pi-2m1.27.57, the scheduler created checkpoint commit 07db07d7f4c49f39bfe3d4c215c69c48f82b7fea before worker start, but the successor bundle lacked artifacts/checkpoint.json when the worker inspected it. The worker reconstructed the artifact from Git and Beads evidence. Investigate whether run-bundle initialization overwrites or omits checkpoint evidence, and add a regression test that checkpoint.json exists before worker execution.
acceptance_criteria: A continuation/reset witness run has artifacts/checkpoint.json present before worker execution; it records baseline/checkpoint SHA, status/diff, predecessor, and approval; regression coverage proves bundle initialization cannot erase it.

title: Allow evidence-requiring review workers to run safe non-mutating verification commands
description: E2E continuation review pi-2m1.27.32 reported that it could not execute the documented pytest command because review workers received only read/grep/find/ls. Decide whether review actions that explicitly require command evidence should receive the same safe bash-only tool profile currently used for verify actions, without edit/write tools; preserve ordinary read-only review restrictions and record tool capability in invocation/live status.
acceptance_criteria: A review task that explicitly requires command/test evidence can safely run documented non-mutating commands without edit/write tools; ordinary reviews remain restricted; coverage proves no write tools are exposed; E2E review report includes real command output rather than a tooling caveat.

title: Investigate Gemma4 tool-call invocation formatting before demoting model capability
description:
User noted Gemma4 has native tool-calling capability, so the unresolved tool-call markup seen in E2E should not be treated as proof that Gemma4 cannot use tools.

Observed in pi-2m1.27.7:
- selected model: openrouter-localish/google/gemma-4-31b-it
- worker had tools read,bash,grep,find,ls
- response artifact contained raw markup: `<|tool_call>call:ls{path:"..."}<tool_call|>`
- run was correctly failed by unresolved-tool-call detection.

Important nuance: this may be a bug in how Pi invokes Gemma4 through OpenRouter/localish/completions, how tool schemas are presented, or how streamed tool calls are parsed, rather than a lack of native Gemma4 tool support.

Do not permanently demote Gemma4 tool capability without investigating invocation format/parser compatibility.
acceptance_criteria:
- Compare Gemma4 tool-call invocation format expected by the provider/model against Pi's actual request/stream parser.
- Reproduce with a minimal tool-call prompt and a simple read/ls tool.
- Determine whether failure is model behavior, provider adapter formatting, stream parsing, or tool schema mismatch.
- If fixable, add regression test and restore Gemma4 eligibility for tool-requiring workers.
- If not fixable in this venue, record venue-specific routing constraint rather than model-family-wide demotion.
notes: Blocked for later explicit investigation/approval. This finding should not be auto-dispatched during the Edabit E2E: Gemma4 has native tool-calling capability, so investigate provider/invocation formatting before demoting model capability.

Options: approve-cleanup, defer.

Scope: only the local epic worktree `.worktrees/epic/pi-2m1`; no main-checkout source edits, commit, push, merge, branch/worktree deletion, Beads history rewrite, hooks, launch agents, or secrets access.

Reversibility: uncommitted worktree changes are not easily recoverable after reset/clean unless a diff artifact is saved first.

title: Fix runner tick timeout/misleading not-running response for slow or blocked dispatch
description: Critical bootstrap usability bug observed live: running `pi/agent/bin/agnt work runner tick --json --limit 1` returned `connected=false`, `detail=timed out`, `running=false`, `status=not-running`, while the Pi gateway still reported the runner as `running active=0`. Root cause likely: the client has a 2s timeout and `/v1/tick` executes scheduler work synchronously, so a slow dispatch/blocker path is reported as if the daemon is not running. This gives the operator the wrong next action and forces manual shell debugging.
acceptance_criteria: TDD evidence recorded. A regression test simulates a slow `/v1/tick` or slow dispatch path and verifies the CLI/gateway does not report `not-running` merely because tick exceeded the short request timeout. The tick path should either return quickly with an accepted/background job id, use a longer/operation-specific timeout, expose active/in-progress status, or return an accurate timeout status with a safe next action. Existing status checks must still correctly report genuinely missing/stale services. Relevant runner client/service/scheduler tests pass.
close_reason: Fixed by pi-2m1.19: runner client timeout payload no longer misreports a live slow service as not-running; scheduler/action dispatch fixes also prevent malformed ready items from shadowing valid work.

title: Make runner daemon autonomously dispatch approved ready work without manual tick command
description: Critical bootstrap reliability bug: a healthy project-local runner service can sit at `running active=0` indefinitely because `agnt work daemon serve` starts the HTTP service and joins its thread, but does not run a scheduler loop. Work only advances when an operator manually runs `agnt work runner tick --json`. This violates the harness goal: once approved work is ready and the runner is healthy, it should pick up bounded work automatically without requiring repeated shell commands.
acceptance_criteria: TDD evidence recorded. A regression test proves daemon/service mode schedules bounded ticks on its configured interval while running and accepting work. A healthy runner with approved ready work transitions from running idle to an active run or a durable blocked/error state without a manual `runner tick` command. The scheduler respects paused/draining state, concurrency/active run limits, dry-run semantics if applicable, idempotency, and does not dispatch while leases are draining or service is stopping. Relevant runner scheduler/service/client tests pass.
close_reason: Fixed by pi-2m1.19: daemon serve now starts an autonomous scheduler loop and reports schedulerEnabled; stale worktree blocker pi-76g superseded.

title: Make harness self-recover from drained runner state without manual shell reset
description: High-priority bootstrap reliability bug from live use: after normal Pi restart, the project-local runner can be running but stuck in `draining` with `acceptingNewWork=false`, requiring manual shell commands to stop, archive `.pi/runner`, restart, or POST `/v1/resume`. This makes the harness unable to do work. Root causes observed: the orchestrator extension attaches a new `pi-tui` lease to a runner service that is already drained/draining; startup `ensureDaemon` treats a connected service as good without checking `draining`/`acceptingNewWork`; `/v1/resume`/`runner_resume` appears not to clear `draining`, so a resume request does not restore accepting-new-work state. Fix the runner/orchestrator behavior so a normal Pi restart recovers automatically into a healthy idle service, with no manual shell reset.
acceptance_criteria: TDD evidence recorded. A regression test reproduces a running runner state with `draining=true`, `acceptingNewWork=false`, and/or a stale/new `pi-tui` lease on startup. After the fix, normal orchestrator startup either resumes/undrains the service or safely restarts it so status becomes running, non-draining, acceptingNewWork=true, active=0 without manual shell commands. `/v1/resume` clears `draining` and drain metadata or an explicit undrain endpoint exists and is used. Shutdown remains idempotent and still drains/stops when appropriate. Relevant orchestrator, runner service, runner protocol/client, gateway visibility tests pass. No runtime state reset is required for normal restart recovery.
close_reason: Fixed by pi-2m1.19: runner resume/startup now clears drained state and orchestrator startup resumes or upgrades unhealthy runner services without manual shell reset.

title: Recover runner from stale draining state after harness restart
description: After restarting Pi normally with extensions/no bypass, the orchestrator extension/gateway path is alive but ticket_gateway runner_status reports `draining active=0`, so the runner is not ready to accept work. Recover the project-local runner to a healthy idle state by stopping/draining as needed, archiving stale ignored `.pi/runner/` runtime state if necessary, restarting the runner, and verifying it reports running, non-draining, accepting new work, active=0.
acceptance_criteria: Runner status reports running, non-draining, accepting new work, active=0. Recovery affects only ignored project-local runtime state under `.pi/runner/` and runner process lifecycle; no source edits, commit, push, merge, deploy, hook/launch-agent install, tracked file deletion, secrets, or Beads history rewrite. Outcome and commands are recorded as evidence.
close_reason: Recovered earlier to running active=0; durable source fix in pi-2m1.19 now prevents this stale draining recovery from requiring manual shell reset.

title: Execute approved orphaned-lease harness repair through runner
description: Approved execution bead for the harness repair requested by the user. Supersedes/implements follow-up pi-2m1.13 if the runner requires approved metadata at creation time. Fix orchestrator-service startup/shutdown orphaned lease race using TDD: add behavioral regression coverage for shutdown while lease attachment is in flight or just accepted, then change lease tracking/cleanup so pending/accepted leases are released idempotently and polling cannot start after shutdown. Root cause: attachLease() records state.leaseId only after POST returns, allowing session_shutdown abort after runner accepts the lease but before local state can release it.
acceptance_criteria: Execution path: dispatch-through-runner first; temporary repair mode only if runner dispatch fails. TDD evidence recorded: a behavioral regression test fails before the fix and passes after. The test simulates shutdown while async startup/lease attachment is in flight or just accepted. After shutdown, no polling starts, no fetch-failed/error widget is shown for expected drain/stop, drain remains idempotent, and no pending/accepted lease remains orphaned. Existing orchestrator extension tests and relevant runner protocol/service tests pass. Edits limited to pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator-service.ts, tests/test_orchestrator_extension.py, and run evidence.
notes:
Repair-mode fallback used after runner dispatch remained idle (runner running active=0) and user restarted Pi with temporary bash-enabled scope.

TDD RED: `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_orchestrator_extension.py -q` failed as expected before production change: `test_orchestrator_extension_releases_lease_accepted_during_inflight_attach` reported `orphaned accepted lease ... state={"shutdownStarted":false}`.

Fix: `orchestrator-service.ts` now tracks `pendingLeaseId` before posting the lease, lets `releaseLease()` release either active or pending leases, and cleans up a lease if attach completes or rejects after shutdown/release cleared the pending id.

GREEN/focused: `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_orchestrator_extension.py -q` -> 9 passed. Relevant runner checks: `.venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_orchestrator_extension.py tests/test_runner_protocol.py tests/test_runner_service.py tests/test_runner_client.py -q` -> 22 passed.

Regression/project checks: `scripts/check-pi-config.sh && .venv/bin/python -m pytest tests/ -q` -> config PASS and 182 passed. `bash -n scripts/*.sh && pi/agent/bin/agnt eval run routing-smoke && pi/agent/bin/agnt eval run role-context-smoke` -> both evals passed. `git diff --check` -> PASS.

Changed source/test paths within approved scope: `pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator-service.ts`, `tests/test_orchestrator_extension.py`.
close_reason: Fixed orphaned lease startup/shutdown race with pending-lease tracking and behavioral RED/GREEN regression evidence.

title: Fix orchestrator-service orphaned lease startup/shutdown race
description: Follow-up from review of pi-2m1.12. Root cause: during async startup, attachLease generates a lease id but records it only after the POST returns. If session_shutdown aborts the fetch after the runner service accepts the lease but before the client stores state.leaseId, shutdown cannot release the lease and drain can leave the project-local runner stuck draining with a stale pi-tui lease. Add behavioral regression coverage for startup/shutdown overlap and fix lease tracking/cleanup so accepted or pending leases are released idempotently and status polling cannot start after shutdown.
acceptance_criteria: TDD evidence recorded: a behavioral regression test fails before the fix and passes after. The test simulates shutdown while async startup/lease attachment is in flight or just accepted. After shutdown, no polling starts, no fetch-failed/error widget is shown for expected drain/stop, drain remains idempotent, and no pending/accepted lease remains orphaned. Existing orchestrator extension tests and relevant runner protocol/service tests pass. Edits are limited to pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator-service.ts, tests/test_orchestrator_extension.py, and run evidence unless separately approved.

Non-goals: no remote dashboard yet, no global multi-project daemon, no auto-push/merge/deploy, no Beads replacement, and no host-level launch agent/hook install without separate approval.

title: Implement agnt lessons client for lesson server
description:
Context: A FastAPI lesson aggregation server now exists at /lesson and /lessons and production URL https://pi-lessons.st5ve.com has been smoke-tested.

Problem: Agents still lack a first-class way to capture local lessons, push them to the server, pull/triage remote lessons, and turn accepted lessons into Beads work in this repo.

Suggested Improvement: Add agnt lessons with capture, inbox, push, pull, and triage/draft-beads flows. Use JSONL with uuid/date/hostname/project/project_dir provenance and configurable AGNT_LESSONS_URL.

Acceptance Criteria:
- agnt lessons capture writes sanitized JSONL to a local runtime inbox.
- agnt lessons push posts JSONL to AGNT_LESSONS_URL/lesson and marks or archives pushed items without losing data.
- agnt lessons pull fetches AGNT_LESSONS_URL/lessons as JSONL with filters.
- agnt lessons triage can list/group candidates and draft Beads creation commands or create Beads only with explicit flag.
- Tests cover local JSONL IO, remote HTTP mocking, provenance defaults, and redaction boundaries.
- Docs connect lessons to SELF-IMPROVEMENT and retrospective workflows.
close_reason: Added agnt lessons capture/inbox/push/pull/triage client with JSONL provenance, redaction, remote server sync, and tests.

title: Implement agnt doctor operational preflight
description:
Context: Agents can become unpredictable when the runtime environment diverges from assumptions.

Problem: Broken dependencies, provider env vars, wrong Node version, missing Beads/Docker/Pi binaries, or model/provider failures are discovered late during agent work.

Suggested Improvement: Add agnt doctor as a deterministic operational preflight with JSON output, strict mode, actionable remediation, and Node LTS/nvm/home-management checks. Integrate it into agnt invoke/work paths as a preflight/warning surface.

Acceptance Criteria:
- agnt doctor reports checks with status, severity, evidence, and suggested actions.
- Checks include core binaries, git root, Python, Pi, Beads, Node LTS/nvm manager detection, provider env vars, configured model targets, and project verification command presence where practical.
- Node remediation respects home management conventions and never edits shell/home files without explicit repair command and confirmation-oriented output.
- Tests cover report schema, node-version scenarios, env-var redaction, and nonzero strict behavior.
- Docs explain when agents should run doctor after repeated tool/env failures.
close_reason: Added agnt doctor operational preflight with Node LTS/provider/config checks, strict JSON reporting, and invoke/work preflight hooks.

title: Spike FastAPI lesson aggregation server
description:
Context: User wants an immediately deployable Docker Compose FastAPI service to aggregate lessons learned from Pi agents.

Problem: Lessons currently have no central intake server; agents need a simple JSONL-compatible API with provenance.

Suggested Improvement: Add a small deployable FastAPI + Postgres lesson server with /lesson and /lessons endpoints, Docker Compose on an external Caddy network, and local tests/fixture support.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Docker Compose defines FastAPI API, Postgres dependency, external Caddy network, and internal DB network.
- API supports POST /lesson for JSON or JSONL upsert by uuid, GET /lesson by uuid, PATCH /lesson by uuid, and GET /lessons as JSONL.
- Lesson schema includes uuid, date, hostname, project/project_dir, summary/status/tags/payload fields.
- Local tests can run without Docker Compose or with an isolated fixture.
- README documents deployment and Caddy proxy assumptions.
close_reason: Added FastAPI + Postgres lesson server with Docker Compose, JSON/JSONL API, test endpoints, README, and pytest coverage.

To avoid repeated prompts, authorize the orchestrator to detect and stop only duplicate daemons whose root is inside this epic worktree, provided the main daemon is healthy and the duplicate has active=0. title: What rollback threat boundary should the approval authority enforce?
description:
Beads-backed question request.

Question: What rollback threat boundary should the approval authority enforce?

Context:
Independent Terra review found that restoring a complete pre-redemption snapshot of runner state, keyring, journal, and anchor makes all local MACs mutually valid and permits replay. No project-local file design can prevent a malicious same-user actor who can atomically roll back every authority file and signing key. It also found a separately fixable HIGH path issue: the HTTP bearer token and parent directories are not descriptor-anchored. The new contained-worker boundary removes bash/process access and denies `.pi/runner`, `.beads`, secrets, and outside-root paths, so a model worker cannot perform full-store rollback. We need decide whether the authority threat model is contained model workers (simple/cross-platform) or malicious same-user filesystem control (requires an external trust anchor and platform tradeoffs).

Options:
- Contained-worker threat boundary (recommended): protect against model/worker forgery, replay, path escape, and partial rollback; treat full same-user rollback of every runner authority file/key as out of scope, document it, and fix token/parent descriptor paths
- macOS Keychain monotonic anchor: store the journal generation/digest outside project files in Keychain and fail closed if unavailable; stronger same-user rollback resistance but macOS-specific and operationally more complex
- External per-user state anchor: store generation/digest under a separate mode-0700 user state root denied to workers; cross-platform and prevents project snapshot rollback, but not a malicious same-user attacker who rolls back both roots
- Pause and design a stronger platform-neutral authority service before continuing

Requested default: Contained-worker threat boundary (recommended): protect against model/worker forgery, replay, path escape, and partial rollback; treat full same-user rollback of every runner authority file/key as out of scope, document it, and fix token/parent descriptor paths
Requesting run: runner-pi-2m1.38.44-20260713000318
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.43

Approval preview:
- Action: Choose the trust boundary for rollback/replay protection before the final authority primitive correction.
- Scope: Decision only. No source, checkpoint, deployment, runtime, Git, secret, or external-state mutation.
- Consequences: The recommended option keeps the architecture simple and cross-platform while explicitly relying on worker containment; Keychain provides stronger rollback resistance at the cost of platform coupling and operational complexity.
- Reversibility: The boundary can later be strengthened before deployment through a new design/migration. No mutation occurs from answering.
- Closeout path: Record the selected boundary, implement descriptor-safe token/parent handling and any selected anchor, add behavioral tests/docs, independently re-review, then proceed to UI/scheduler/program-authority integration.
notes: Beads-backed question resolved as answered: Answered in Pi UI: Contained-worker threat boundary (recommended): protect against model/worker forgery, replay, path escape, and partial rollback; treat full same-user rollback of every runner authority file/key as out of scope, document it, and fix token/parent descriptor paths

We need decide whether the authority threat model is contained model workers (simple/cross-platform) or malicious same-user filesystem control (requires an external trust anchor and platform tradeoffs).

options: Contained-worker threat boundary (recommended): protect against model/worker forgery, replay, path escape, and partial rollback; treat full same-user rollback of every runner authority file/key as out of scope, document it, and fix token/parent descriptor paths

macOS Keychain monotonic anchor: store the journal generation/digest outside project files in Keychain and fail closed if unavailable; stronger same-user rollback resistance but macOS-specific and operationally more complex

External per-user state anchor: store generation/digest under a separate mode-0700 user state root denied to workers; cross-platform and prevents project snapshot rollback, but not a malicious same-user attacker who rolls back both roots
Pause and design a stronger platform-neutral authority service before continuing

preview.scope: Decision only. No source, checkpoint, deployment, runtime, Git, secret, or external-state mutation.
preview.action: Choose the trust boundary for rollback/replay protection before the final authority primitive correction.
preview.closeoutPath: Record the selected boundary, implement descriptor-safe token/parent handling and any selected anchor, add behavioral tests/docs, independently re-review, then proceed to UI/scheduler/program-authority integration.
preview.consequences: The recommended option keeps the architecture simple and cross-platform while explicitly relying on worker containment; Keychain provides stronger rollback resistance at the cost of platform coupling and operational complexity.
preview.reversibility: The boundary can later be strengthened before deployment through a new design/migration. No mutation occurs from answering.

approval.question: What rollback threat boundary should the approval authority enforce?

title: Grant bounded rolling WIP-checkpoint authority for security-kernel completion so critical fixes can iterate without repeated approval prompts?
description:
Beads-backed approval request.

Question: Grant bounded rolling WIP-checkpoint authority for security-kernel completion so critical fixes can iterate without repeated approval prompts?

Context:
The security kernel is intentionally self-hosting: each failed write run leaves a dirty worktree that the normal runner refuses to continue without a local checkpoint. Two independent reviews correctly found high-severity issues and stopped deployment. Requiring a new human checkpoint exception after every RED iteration defeats the Source Autonomy goal. This proposed authority is narrow: up to five additional local WIP checkpoints, only for the security-kernel paths and corrective tests, each explicitly marked insecure/not deployable, solely to permit the next normal-runner corrective slice. It expires immediately when independent review reports no critical/high issue, after five checkpoints, on scope expansion, or on revocation.

Options:
- Grant up to five security-kernel WIP checkpoints under strict no-deploy rules
- Do not grant rolling WIP authority; require approval for each checkpoint

Requested default: Grant up to five security-kernel WIP checkpoints under strict no-deploy rules
Requesting run: unknown
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.29

Approval preview:
- Action: Allow the scheduler to checkpoint dirty RED security-kernel slices before bounded corrective successors without asking each time.
- Scope: Only approval/gateway/orchestration/runner/invocation/contained-worker files, their focused existing or newly created tests, and mapped authority/runner docs in epic/pi-2m1-38. Maximum five WIP commits. Every commit records predecessor, exact paths, test status, known findings, and `NOT DEPLOYABLE`. No unrelated source or Tasks 3–14 implementation may use this exception.
- Consequences: Local history may contain several knowingly incomplete security intermediates, improving auditability and allowing normal-runner continuation. Nothing is pushed, merged, or deployed.
- Reversibility: Revoke at any time. The local WIP commits can later be reverted under separately approved non-destructive history work.
- Closeout path: After each WIP, run one bounded corrective task and independent adversarial review. Stop immediately for scope expansion outside security paths, exhausted five-commit allowance, unresolved design fork, budget gate, or any deployment/runtime action. A secure checkpoint requires full verification and no critical/high review findings.

close_reason: Beads-backed approval resolved as approved: Approved in Pi UI

description:
Beads-backed approval request.

Question: Approve stopping the rogue duplicate runner daemon rooted inside the epic worktree?

Context:
Root cause is now confirmed: two autonomous daemons are running against the same Beads graph. Healthy main daemon PID 11795 is rooted at `/Users/hays/Projects/pi-setup`; rogue PID 29790 is rooted at `/Users/hays/Projects/pi-setup/.worktrees/epic/pi-2m1-38`. The rogue daemon derives a nested `.worktrees/epic/pi-2m1-38` path, fails to find it, creates repeated false blockers, and writes events under the worktree. Fresh main-daemon dry-run correctly reports `would_start`. Stopping only PID 29790 is necessary; queued work is durable and main daemon remains online.

- Leave both daemons running and keep task blocked

Question: Approve one controlled idle runner restart to load the verified worktree-resolution code and release the security-kernel task?

Context:

description:
Beads-backed approval request.

Question: Grant durable Source Autonomy for architecture-remediation Tasks 3–14 under the immutable approved program manifest?

Context:
Decision pi-h0x6 selected Source Autonomy. This approval binds that choice to epic pi-2m1.38, branch epic/pi-2m1-38, plan checkpoint 386da32d462811fa4cc7657819c84ea99514c60d, and plan SHA-256 77beb42b83c7e2b0a18ee0e8337b65e76cb4326a03f876b1a580464c64b361d4. The security kernel must treat this as a bounded program authority: child tasks may narrow action, writeSet, effects, budget, and scope but may not expand them. It expires when Tasks 3–14 close, the epic is superseded, the branch/worktree changes identity, the manifest hash changes, or you revoke it. Source autonomy covers normal-runner source/test/docs/config/script work, Beads planning/closeout, model reviews, deterministic verification, and verified local checkpoint commits. It does not cover deployment or live runtime lifecycle.

Options:
- Grant Source Autonomy for Tasks 3–14 under this manifest
- Do not grant broad authority; retain per-task approvals

Requested default: Grant Source Autonomy for Tasks 3–14 under this manifest
Requesting run: unknown
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.27

Approval preview:
- Action: Authorize autonomous creation, dispatch, correction, review, verification, and local checkpointing of Tasks 3–14 as strict subsets of the hashed program manifest.
- Scope: Epic pi-2m1.38 only; branch epic/pi-2m1-38 only; Tasks 3–14 only; tracked source/tests/docs/config/scripts and reversible legacy removals explicitly mapped by the plan; local commits only after fresh verification. Includes bounded adjacent-file expansion only when mechanically necessary to satisfy a mapped acceptance criterion, recorded before edit with reason and independently reviewed. Budget envelope remains per-epic marginalUsd=$250, retailEquivalentUsd=$1,000, maxPlannedRunRetailUsd=$50; any reached/unknown budget gate stops before dispatch.
- Consequences: Routine child tasks will no longer request individual approval. The agent may create multiple local commits and iteratively fix/review code. No live configuration changes occur. Failures, security findings, or unexpected scope changes remain durable and auditable.
- Reversibility: Revoke or narrow this authority at any time. Local commits can be reverted through a separately approved action; no push or deployment is authorized.
- Closeout path: Every child records programAuthorityRef, manifest hash, exact effective writeSet/effects, tests, cost, and checkpoint evidence. Pause for scope expansion outside the manifest, unresolved design forks, critical/high review findings, budget gates, or always-ask actions. Always ask before deployment to ~/.pi, runner start/restart/drain/force-stop, push/fetch/merge/rebase/reset/clean/amend, destructive cleanup, branch/worktree deletion or creation, hooks/launch agents, secrets, external network side effects, remote lesson operations, or Edabit/unrelated changes.

title: What autonomy level should govern architecture-remediation Tasks 3–14?
description:
Beads-backed question request.

Question: What autonomy level should govern architecture-remediation Tasks 3–14?

Context:
You want fewer repeated approvals while preserving meaningful oversight. The durable program plan defines Tasks 3–14, candidate writeSets, dependency order, tests, migrations, local checkpoints, and separate runtime gates. Current human-only dispatch requires target-specific approvals; the security kernel should therefore add a bounded program-authority record whose immutable scope/hash is verified for every child task. Child tasks may narrow but never expand that authority. Any scope expansion, failed security boundary, budget gate, destructive/remote effect, or unresolved design fork still escalates. Current architecture work is not deployed; runner is healthy and idle; retail-equivalent observed cost is about $23 and budget enforcement remains under construction.

Options:
- Source autonomy (recommended): autonomously create/dispatch Tasks 3–14, edit tracked source/tests/docs/config/scripts within the approved program scope, run reviews, and create verified local checkpoints; still ask before deployment, runner restart/drain, push/merge, destructive cleanup, branch/worktree deletion, hooks/launch agents, secrets, or external network effects
- Staging autonomy: everything in Source autonomy plus deploy verified tracked Pi config to ~/.pi and perform controlled idle runner restarts for bounded acceptance; still ask before push/merge, destructive cleanup, branch/worktree deletion, hooks/launch agents, secrets, or external network effects
- Planning/review autonomy only: autonomously plan, create Beads, run read-only reviews and tests, but ask before every source edit, local commit, deployment, or runtime action
- Custom boundaries: specify different allowed and always-ask effects

Requested default: Source autonomy (recommended): autonomously create/dispatch Tasks 3–14, edit tracked source/tests/docs/config/scripts within the approved program scope, run reviews, and create verified local checkpoints; still ask before deployment, runner restart/drain, push/merge, destructive cleanup, branch/worktree deletion, hooks/launch agents, secrets, or external network effects
Requesting run: unknown
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.27

Approval preview:
- Action: Choose a durable autonomy envelope for Tasks 3–14; a separate approval will bind the selected envelope and immutable program manifest.
- Scope: Decision only. It does not itself authorize source, Git, deployment, runtime, remote, destructive, secret, or network effects.
- Consequences: The selected envelope determines which routine steps proceed without repeated prompts and which always escalate. Child work must remain a strict subset of the approved manifest.
- Reversibility: You may revoke or narrow the program authority at any time. Expansion requires a new human approval.
- Closeout path: Record the choice, present one concise final approval with scope/consequences/reversibility, then bind its decision bead and manifest hash into the security-kernel/program-authority implementation.

title: How should trusted human approval authority cross the Pi UI-to-runner boundary now that independent review proved CLI/Beads provenance is forgeable?
description:
Beads-backed question request.

Question: How should trusted human approval authority cross the Pi UI-to-runner boundary now that independent review proved CLI/Beads provenance is forgeable?

Context:
Independent security review pi-2m1.38.24 found a deployment-blocking bypass: any same-user process with `agnt` or Beads access can supply `--resolver-kind human-ui --resolver-session ...`, create matching decision/target metadata, and satisfy the current scheduler verifier. Field shape and rereading Beads cannot prove a human acted. This creates a circular dependency with Task 2 because current write workers have broad bash/environment/filesystem access. A durable authority boundary therefore needs both an unforgeable runner-owned capability and containment that prevents workers from reading/calling that capability directly. The review also found a smaller undefined `outcome` TypeScript regression to fix in the same bounded repair.

Options:
- Combine Task 1 authority and the minimum Task 2 containment prerequisite: runner-owned authenticated local approval endpoint/capability, human UI confirmation, no trusted outcomes in general CLI/model tools, and workers denied approval token/socket/Beads authority access (recommended)
- Use a one-shot signed approval capability issued after UI confirmation, with its verification key owned by the runner and minimum worker containment in the same slice
- Pause implementation and produce a focused protocol design/review before more source changes

Requested default: Combine Task 1 authority and the minimum Task 2 containment prerequisite: runner-owned authenticated local approval endpoint/capability, human UI confirmation, no trusted outcomes in general CLI/model tools, and workers denied approval token/socket/Beads authority access (recommended)
Requesting run: runner-pi-2m1.38.24-20260712012223
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.23

Approval preview:
- Action: Choose the non-forgeable human-approval protocol and its minimum containment boundary before further implementation.
- Scope: Decision only. No source edit, checkpoint, deployment, restart, runtime mutation, or Git operation is authorized by this question.
- Consequences: The recommended option merges only the security-circularity portion of Tasks 1 and 2 so a write worker cannot mint or steal approval authority; remaining Task 2 containment continues afterward.
- Reversibility: The protocol can be revised before deployment; no mutation occurs from answering.
- Closeout path: Record the human decision, create a newly scoped normal-runner TDD bead, independently security-review it, then checkpoint only after both authority and containment evidence pass.

title: Approve completing runner-root propagation in runner.py, with scoped checkpoint, deployment, and one idle restart?
description:
Beads-backed approval request.

Question: Approve completing runner-root propagation in runner.py, with scoped checkpoint, deployment, and one idle restart?

Context:
The prior scheduler fix was bypassed by runner.py: runner_tick had its own default worktree_snapshot_for_bead object and always passed it explicitly, preventing runner_scheduler from binding the configured root. RED reproduced the wrapper bypass; GREEN makes runner.py default to None so scheduler owns root binding. Verification passed: 38 focused tests and 284 full tests. This is the final call-chain correction, not a new policy.

Options:
- Approve scoped checkpoint, deploy, and one idle runner restart
- Keep the verified wrapper fix undeployed

Requested default: Approve scoped checkpoint, deploy, and one idle runner restart
Requesting run: unknown
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.14

Approval preview:
- Action: Commit runner.py plus its behavioral test, deploy tracked config, restart the idle runner, resolve only the stale pre-fix blocker, and observe pi-2m1.38.14 dispatch.
- Scope: Only pi/agent/bin/agnt_lib/runner.py and tests/test_runner.py. No push, merge, destructive Git, worktree/branch deletion, Beads history rewrite, hooks, launch agents, secrets, runtime deletion, Edabit, or unrelated changes.
- Consequences: runner_tick no longer overrides scheduler root-aware worktree resolution. The runner briefly restarts.
- Reversibility: Revert the scoped checkpoint, redeploy prior config, and restart.
- Closeout path: Record SHA/paths, deploy byte-match, restart healthy, resolve stale blocker pi-m6qb/pi-9ok3 successor only, observe autonomous plan run, then disable repair-tools.

Question: Approve the final runner-root worktree resolution fix, scoped checkpoint, deployment, and one idle restart?

Context:
The canonical ID-only fix was correct but exposed a second root cause: runner_scheduler called the default worktree resolver without the configured project root, so a daemon started from another cwd searched for a nested nonexistent `.worktrees` directory. RED/GREEN now binds the default resolver to runner_scheduler_tick(root=...). Custom test resolvers remain unchanged. Verification passed: 17 scheduler tests and 283 full tests. This explains repeated stale worktree blockers despite production-equivalent direct resolution succeeding.

Options:
- Approve scoped checkpoint, deploy, and one idle runner restart
- Keep the verified runner-root fix undeployed

Requested default: Approve scoped checkpoint, deploy, and one idle runner restart
Requesting run: unknown
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.14

Approval preview:
- Action: Commit runner_scheduler.py and its behavioral test, deploy tracked config, restart the idle runner once, resolve the already-created stale blocker, and observe pi-2m1.38.14 dispatch.
- Scope: Only pi/agent/bin/agnt_lib/runner_scheduler.py and tests/test_runner_scheduler.py. No push, merge, destructive Git, worktree/branch deletion, Beads history rewrite, hooks, launch agents, secrets, runtime artifact deletion, Edabit, or unrelated changes.
- Consequences: Default worktree resolution becomes independent of daemon process cwd and consistently uses the configured project root. The runner briefly restarts.
- Reversibility: Revert the scoped local checkpoint, redeploy prior config, and restart.
- Closeout path: Record SHA/paths, deploy byte-match, restart healthy, resolve only stale pre-fix blocker pi-c0i2 or its successor, observe normal autonomous run bundle, then disable repair-tools.

title: Approve checkpointing and deploying the canonical epic-worktree resolver fix, then restarting the idle runner once to release the architecture plan?
description:
Beads-backed approval request.

Question: Approve checkpointing and deploying the canonical epic-worktree resolver fix, then restarting the idle runner once to release the architecture plan?

Context:
After scheduler fairness was deployed, pi-2m1.38.14 became visible but the resolver falsely reported its existing worktree missing. Root cause: epic_worktree_spec appended mutable parent-title text, while the audited worktree uses the canonical ID-only path `.worktrees/epic/pi-2m1-38`. Behavioral RED/GREEN now makes worktree identity depend only on epicId. Verification passed: 10 focused worktree tests and 282 full tests. This directly implements the previously recorded canonical ID-only naming decision and is the remaining activation blocker.

Options:
- Approve scoped checkpoint, deploy, and one idle runner restart
- Keep the verified resolver fix undeployed

Requested default: Approve scoped checkpoint, deploy, and one idle runner restart
Requesting run: unknown
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.14

Approval preview:
- Action: Commit worktree_policy.py and its focused test, deploy tracked Pi config, restart the idle project runner, resolve the stale worktree blocker if recreated, and observe pi-2m1.38.14 autonomous dispatch.
- Scope: Only pi/agent/bin/agnt_lib/worktree_policy.py and tests/test_worktree_policy.py are committed. No main edits, push, merge, destructive Git, worktree/branch deletion, Beads history rewrite, hooks, launch agents, secrets, Edabit changes, runtime artifact deletion, or unrelated cleanup.
- Consequences: All epic worktree paths and branches become deterministic from epicId only; mutable titles no longer alter resolution. The live runner briefly restarts.
- Reversibility: Revert the scoped local checkpoint, redeploy the prior config, and restart. Existing worktrees are not renamed or removed.
- Closeout path: Record baseline/checkpoint SHA and paths, deploy byte-match, restart healthy, resolve only the now-stale worktree gate through human UI, confirm normal autonomous run bundle for pi-2m1.38.14, then disable repair-tools.

title: Approve checkpointing and deploying the scheduler fairness fix, followed by one controlled runner restart so the blocked architecture-remediation plan can dispatch autonomously?
description:
Beads-backed approval request.

Question: Approve checkpointing and deploying the scheduler fairness fix, followed by one controlled runner restart so the blocked architecture-remediation plan can dispatch autonomously?

Context:
Main architecture remediation is blocked before planning: pi-2m1.38.14 remains unclaimed while the runner is healthy and idle because runner_scheduler._ready_items invokes the default limited `bd ready` window and filters candidates only afterward. RED proved the eligible older item is hidden; GREEN changes the request to `bd ready --limit 0` before filtering. All 16 scheduler tests pass. Scope is exactly runner_scheduler.py and tests/test_runner_scheduler.py. This is the prerequisite to resume the normal-runner remediation program without manual ticks or repeated repair mode.

Options:
- Approve checkpoint, deploy, and controlled runner restart
- Keep the verified fix undeployed

Requested default: Approve checkpoint, deploy, and controlled runner restart
Requesting run: unknown
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.14

Approval preview:
- Action: Create one scoped local checkpoint commit for the scheduler fairness fix, deploy tracked Pi config to ~/.pi, restart the project-local runner once, and observe whether pi-2m1.38.14 is autonomously claimed.
- Scope: Only pi/agent/bin/agnt_lib/runner_scheduler.py and tests/test_runner_scheduler.py are committed. Deployment copies tracked Pi config. No main-checkout edits, push, merge, destructive Git, branch/worktree deletion, Beads history rewrite, hooks, launch agents, secrets, Edabit changes, or unrelated cleanup.
- Consequences: The scheduler requests the complete Beads-ready set before filtering, allowing eligible older work to be considered past leading unmanaged or approval-waiting items. The live runner briefly restarts, then should resume autonomous dispatch.
- Reversibility: Revert the scoped local checkpoint and redeploy the prior tracked config; the restart itself is transient.
- Closeout path: Record baseline/checkpoint SHA and exact paths, deploy and verify byte match, restart runner, confirm healthy scheduler status, then observe durable autonomous claim/run artifacts for pi-2m1.38.14 without manual tick.

title: Approve checkpointing and deploying the verified always-available orchestrator repair broker, then reloading Pi and disabling unrestricted repair-tools mode?
description:
Beads-backed approval request.

Question: Approve checkpointing and deploying the verified always-available orchestrator repair broker, then reloading Pi and disabling unrestricted repair-tools mode?

Context:
The five-file change removes PI_ORCHESTRATOR_REPAIR_BROKER and --orchestrator-repair-broker opt-in state while keeping repair only on the orchestrator safe surface. GPT-5.6 Sol evaluation, sandbox/audit protections, worker exclusion, and all protected-effect boundaries remain unchanged. Fresh verification passed: 43 focused tests, 281 full tests, Ruff F821, shell syntax, diff checks, and routing/role-context smokes. This addresses the repeated bootstrap problem: normal orchestration will retain repair without repeatedly enabling unrestricted repair-tools.

Options:
- Approve checkpoint, deploy, reload, and return to normal mode
- Do not deploy; keep verified changes only in the worktree

Requested default: Approve checkpoint, deploy, reload, and return to normal mode
Requesting run: unknown
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.16

Approval preview:
- Action: Create one scoped local checkpoint commit for the five verified files, deploy tracked pi/ config to ~/.pi, reload Pi once, and disable unrestricted repair-tools mode.
- Scope: Only docs/REPAIR-TOOL.md, pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator-service.ts, pi/agent/extensions/repair/index.ts, tests/test_orchestrator_extension.py, and tests/test_repair_extension.py are committed. Deployment copies tracked Pi config. No main-checkout edit, push, merge, destructive Git, branch/worktree deletion, Beads history rewrite, hooks, launch agents, secrets, runner lifecycle mutation, or unrelated cleanup.
- Consequences: After reload, repair is always present on the normal orchestrator safe-tool surface without environment variables or CLI flags; raw bash/edit/write remain absent unless separately enabled. A local checkpoint and live config deployment are created.
- Reversibility: Revert the scoped local commit and redeploy the prior tracked Pi config. No remote history is changed.
- Closeout path: Record baseline/checkpoint SHA and exact committed paths; rerun post-checkpoint checks; deploy with scripts/bootstrap-pi-config.sh --apply; verify deployed files byte-match; reload Pi; confirm normal tool surface retains repair and excludes raw write tools; turn repair-tools off.

This is an integration defect, not a policy refusal. The minimal fix imports/registers the broker from the already auto-loaded orchestrator-service extension and adds behavior coverage. Beads-backed approval request.

Context:
Human decision pi-o527 selected full project write authority after Sol evaluation, with code-enforced nondelegable boundaries for Beads/human approvals, recognized secrets, deployment, project runner control, destructive/remote Git, network, audit evidence, and outside-project effects. The implementation is checkpointed on epic/pi-2m1-38 at a6a93be5599c109609424b5b0f21887d88afad49. Fresh evidence: 38 repair/orchestrator focused tests passed; full suite rerun passed 276 tests; Ruff F821, routing-smoke, role-context-smoke, shell syntax, extension isolated load, and diff checks passed; independent GPT-5.6 Sol review returned OK with no in-scope exploit. One earlier full-suite run had a runner timing failure, but the exact test then passed five consecutive times and the full suite rerun passed. The broker remains disabled unless PI_ORCHESTRATOR_REPAIR_BROKER=1 or --orchestrator-repair-broker is used.

Options:
- Approve deployment, one Pi reload, and the two bounded witnesses
- Approve deployment only; do not run live broker witnesses
- Hold; keep the checkpoint undeployed

Requested default: Approve deployment, one Pi reload, and the two bounded witnesses
Requesting run: pi-2m1.38.16-repair-broker-deployment

- Action: Deploy checkpoint a6a93be repair-broker config from epic/pi-2m1-38 to ~/.pi, reload Pi once, and—if selected—run one harmless approved-output witness plus one protected Beads/authority refusal witness.
- Scope: Deployment updates managed ~/.pi config from the verified tracked worktree. The approved witness may create only broker audit records under project .pi/repair-audit and emit harmless stdout; the refusal witness must not mutate Beads or project content. No source edits, additional commits, push, merge, branch/worktree changes, runner lifecycle changes, destructive Git, hooks, launch agents, secrets, network/install actions, Edabit changes, or unrelated cleanup.
- Consequences: Once enabled in a future session, the orchestrator can ask isolated GPT-5.6 Sol to authorize exact Bash commands with full ordinary project-content write authority. Sol—not a human—judges those project effects. The tool stays hidden by default and retains the documented hard boundaries. Deployment changes live ~/.pi managed files; reload interrupts/replaces the current Pi extension context.
- Reversibility: The deployment can be reverted by redeploying an earlier tracked checkpoint. The feature can be disabled immediately by omitting PI_ORCHESTRATOR_REPAIR_BROKER/--orchestrator-repair-broker. Audit records are retained for review and will not be deleted by this action.
- Closeout path: Verify deployed files byte-match checkpoint a6a93be, ask for/reach one controlled reload, confirm default-hidden and explicit-opt-in behavior, inspect mode-0600/0700 audit artifacts and numeric results for the selected witnesses, record evidence, and close or report any failure without broadening scope.

title: Which exact authority boundary should the GPT-5.6 Sol repair broker have?
description:
Beads-backed question request.

Question: Which exact authority boundary should the GPT-5.6 Sol repair broker have?

Context:
You clarified that the broker should have full write authority and should itself evaluate the justification and impact of allowing each command. This means Sol is a delegated discretionary policy authority, not merely an adviser behind exact-file write restrictions. The remaining choice is whether a small set of human/system authority effects stays structurally nondelegable. The spike remains uncommitted and undeployed.

Options:
- Full project write authority, with code-enforced nondelegable boundaries for Beads/human approvals, secrets, deployment, runner lifecycle, destructive/remote Git, and writes outside the project (recommended interpretation)
- Unrestricted command authority: Sol alone may approve any command/effect available to the process, including system authority, secrets, deployment, lifecycle, remote Git, and outside-project writes
- Full project write authority with a different nondelegable boundary that I will specify

Requested default: Full project write authority, with code-enforced nondelegable boundaries for Beads/human approvals, secrets, deployment, runner lifecycle, destructive/remote Git, and writes outside the project (recommended interpretation)
Requesting run: pi-2m1.38.16-repair-broker-spike
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.16

Approval preview:
- Action: Define the delegated authority boundary for the exceptional repair broker.
- Scope: Affects the uncommitted repair-broker design only. No command execution, deployment, checkpoint, runner restart, or unrelated mutation occurs from this decision.
- Consequences: The recommended option permits arbitrary project-content writes after Sol approval while retaining structural protection around human authority and high-consequence external/system effects. Unrestricted authority would make Sol the sole gate for all process capabilities and materially weakens human-only and secret boundaries.
- Reversibility: The policy can be narrowed before deployment; widening it later requires another explicit decision and verification.
- Closeout path: Record the human choice, rewrite RED tests and docs to that authority model, remove inappropriate exact-file restrictions, preserve audit/hash/timeout controls, run full verification and adversarial review against the chosen boundary, then use the existing separately scoped checkpoint/deployment gates.
notes: Beads-backed question resolved as answered: Answered in Pi UI: Full project write authority, with code-enforced nondelegable boundaries for Beads/human approvals, secrets, deployment, runner lifecycle, destructive/remote Git, and writes outside the project (recommended interpretation)
close_reason: Beads-backed question resolved as answered: Answered in Pi UI: Full project write authority, with code-enforced nondelegable boundaries for Beads/human approvals, secrets, deployment, runner lifecycle, destructive/remote Git, and writes outside the project (recommended interpretation)

title: What mutation authority should the repair broker have, given that arbitrary exact project-file writes can create delayed execution outside the sandbox?
description:
Beads-backed question request.

Question: What mutation authority should the repair broker have, given that arbitrary exact project-file writes can create delayed execution outside the sandbox?

Context:
The bounded direct-exec broker now passes 33 focused tests, 270 full tests, static/smoke checks, and closes prior shell, path, process, audit, secret, and approval-boundary findings. Independent review found a structural remaining issue: a model-approved exact write to an auto-sourced/imported file (demonstrated with .envrc.d/gh.sh) creates persistence and later executes outside the sandbox. Path allowlists and filename regexes cannot reliably classify every project file's future semantics. The current spike is uncommitted and undeployed; runner is idle. A durable authority model is needed rather than another deny-list patch.

Options:
- Typed operation registry (recommended): keep Sol evaluation, but replace arbitrary project writes with reviewed code-defined repair operations and postcondition checks
- Read-only broker: allow bounded diagnostics only; all mutations stay in the normal runner or human-approved workflow
- Human-confirmed writes: Sol may recommend exact writes, but every write-capable request requires interactive human confirmation before execution
- Keep model-only exact writes with best-effort persistence deny-lists (not recommended; known structural bypass risk)

Requested default: Typed operation registry (recommended): keep Sol evaluation, but replace arbitrary project writes with reviewed code-defined repair operations and postcondition checks
Requesting run: pi-2m1.38.16-repair-broker-spike
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.16

Approval preview:
- Action: Choose the repair broker's durable mutation authority model before further implementation.
- Scope: Affects only the uncommitted pi-2m1.38.16 repair-broker design in epic/pi-2m1-38. It does not authorize deployment, checkpoint, runner restart, unrelated source changes, Git history changes, or any command execution.
- Consequences: Typed operations preserve autonomous bounded repairs but require explicit operation implementations; read-only removes mutation utility; human confirmation preserves flexible writes but adds a gate; model-only writes retain a known persistence bypass class.
- Reversibility: This decision can be superseded by a later human decision before deployment. No runtime or repository mutation occurs from answering it.
- Closeout path: Record the selected authority in Beads, update tests/docs first, implement only that model, rerun focused/full verification and independent security review, then seek or use the separately approved checkpoint/deployment gates as applicable.
notes: Beads-backed question resolved as cancelled: Cancelled in Pi UI

title: Approve the recommended fail-closed repair-tool spike, local checkpoint, deployment, and reload?
description:
Beads-backed approval request.

Question: Approve the recommended fail-closed repair-tool spike, local checkpoint, deployment, and reload?

Context:
Pi supports explicit `-e` extensions even with `--no-extensions`, strict TypeBox tools, `pi.exec`, abort signals, and JSON event streams. The safest design is a two-process broker: the orchestrator-only `repair` tool records `{justification, commands, cwd, timeout}`, hashes the immutable request, then launches openai-codex/gpt-5.6-sol with no discovered extensions and only a dedicated terminating `repair_decision` tool. The model supplies only approval and optional reason; trusted extension code executes the exact hash-bound commands if approved and fills `result_stdout`, `result_stderr`, and numeric `result_code` (126 refusal, 124 timeout). Every request gets mode-0600 audit artifacts. The model is not a security boundary: code rejects protected actions before model invocation and again before execution. The tool is installed but disabled by default, enabled only by an explicit orchestrator repair-broker flag. This does not replace human approval for protected actions.

Options:
- Approve constrained opt-in repair-broker spike, checkpoint, deploy, and reload
- Approve evaluator-only dry-run spike with no command execution
- Revise design toward broader authority or always-on availability

Requested default: Approve constrained opt-in repair-broker spike, checkpoint, deploy, and reload
Requesting run: pi-2m1.38.16-repair-broker-spike
Target bead: pi-2m1.38.16

Approval preview:
- Action: Implement and initialize an opt-in GPT-5.6 Sol repair broker in tracked Pi config, verify it, create one scoped local checkpoint on epic/pi-2m1-38, deploy it to ~/.pi, and reload Pi
- Scope: Only `.worktrees/epic/pi-2m1-38/pi/agent/extensions/repair/{index.ts,evaluator.ts,evaluator-system.md}`, `pi/agent/extensions/orchestrator-service.ts`, focused tests, and one repair-tool document; exact final paths may be reduced but not expanded without approval. One local checkpoint commit containing only these verified paths is allowed. Deployment uses that worktree's `scripts/bootstrap-pi-config.sh --apply`, followed by tracked/deployed identity checks and Pi reload. No runner restart/stop/drain, main-checkout source edits, Beads export edits by implementation, push, fetch, merge, reset, clean, rebase, amend, branch/worktree create/delete/move, hooks, launch agents, unrelated cleanup, Edabit changes, or secrets access.
- Consequences: When explicitly enabled, the orchestration session gains one audited exceptional-command surface. It can approve only commands inside a code-defined local project repair envelope. Hard-denied categories include creating/resolving/forging human approvals or editing Beads authority, credential/secret access, network/download/package-install commands, deployment or runner/process lifecycle control, sudo, obfuscation/backgrounding, remote or destructive Git/history/worktree operations, and paths outside the project/audit temp area. The evaluator cannot call bash directly. The spike increases attack surface but remains fail-closed and opt-in; it is not a substitute for human approval.
- Reversibility: The feature is disabled unless explicitly enabled. The local checkpoint remains unpushed and can later be reverted with a separately approved revert commit. Deployment can be replaced by a later verified bootstrap from another tracked checkpoint; audit logs remain as evidence.
- Closeout path: Behavioral RED/GREEN tests cover approved execution, refusal, malformed/empty/conflicting output, protected commands, hash mismatch, timeout/abort, 0600 outputs, audit completeness, and safe-tool/flag gating. Run focused and full project checks, independently review the diff and evaluator prompt, commit only the approved paths, deploy, verify tracked/deployed identity, reload, then run bounded witnesses: harmless approval writes exact stdout/stderr/code and protected-command refusal executes nothing.
notes: Beads-backed approval resolved as approved: Approved in Pi UI
close_reason: Beads-backed approval resolved as approved: Approved in Pi UI
